skill.name: scientific-schematics
skill.description: Generate scientific schematics from natural language prompts using OpenRouter-hosted image models, then review and refine results against document-type quality thresholds.
skill.summary: Generate and review publication-style scientific diagrams from natural language prompts.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: AI review did not find prompt-injection text or confirmed malicious intent. Most static alerts are documentation examples, expected API usage, or image encoding operations. Publication should wait because the wrapper passes the OpenRouter API key as a child process argument.

high_findings.title: API Key Passed Through Child Process Arguments
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The wrapper reads OPENROUTER_API_KEY or --api-key, appends it to the child command, and runs subprocess.run. Command-line secrets can be visible to local process observers. confidence=0.93. confidence_reasoning=The key flow is explicit across argument parsing, command construction, and subprocess execution.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The key flow is explicit across argument parsing, command construction, and subprocess execution.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party API Receives Prompts and Generated Images
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The script sends user prompts, image data, and review requests to OpenRouter endpoints. This is expected functionality, but it can expose confidential research content. confidence=0.86. confidence_reasoning=Network requests, authorization headers, and review image uploads are directly shown in code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network requests, authorization headers, and review image uploads are directly shown in code.

medium_findings.title: Broad Environment File Discovery
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The loader searches current, parent, and package directories for .env files. This is convenient, but it may load unintended secrets from a broader workspace. confidence=0.78. confidence_reasoning=The file search behavior is explicit, but the impact depends on user workspace layout.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file search behavior is explicit, but the impact depends on user workspace layout.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled File Output and Review Logs
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The script creates parent directories, writes image files, copies final output, and writes JSON logs. This is core behavior, but paths are user-controlled and logs can include prompt text. confidence=0.74. confidence_reasoning=The filesystem writes are explicit, while misuse requires a risky output path or sensitive prompt.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem writes are explicit, while misuse requires a risky output path or sensitive prompt.

low_findings.title: Subprocess Use Is Constrained but Still Sensitive
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for shell injection. subprocess.run receives an argument list and does not use shell=True. The remaining risk is credential exposure through argv, captured separately as high severity. confidence=0.89. confidence_reasoning=The command list construction is visible and no shell invocation is used.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command list construction is visible and no shell invocation is used.

low_findings.title: Base64 Operations Are Image Data Handling
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for obfuscation. Base64 decoding extracts image bytes from data URLs returned by the image API. No evidence found that decoded data is executed. confidence=0.91. confidence_reasoning=The decoded bytes are saved as images and used for review, not evaluated as code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The decoded bytes are saved as images and used for review, not evaluated as code.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Examples Trigger Static Alerts
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for many external command findings. Markdown files contain usage examples for running local scripts and setting variables. They are instructions, not executable marketplace code. confidence=0.87. confidence_reasoning=The cited lines are fenced examples or prose in documentation files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are fenced examples or prose in documentation files.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto Alerts Are Terminology Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reviewed files discuss Nano Banana Pro, Gemini, image quality, and scientific diagrams. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptography use for security. confidence=0.82. confidence_reasoning=Targeted review found scientific and model terminology, not cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found scientific and model terminology, not cryptographic operations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential in Process Arguments
dangerous_patterns.description: The wrapper forwards the API key with --api-key when launching the child script. Use inherited environment variables or stdin instead. confidence=0.93. confidence_reasoning=The same command path appears in the high finding.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The same command path appears in the high finding.

dangerous_patterns.title: External AI Service Request With Bearer Token
dangerous_patterns.description: The script sends authorized POST requests to OpenRouter. This is expected, but requires clear user consent and secret handling. confidence=0.86. confidence_reasoning=The Authorization header and request destination are explicit.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Authorization header and request destination are explicit.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Parent Directory .env Loading
dangerous_patterns.description: The script searches multiple parent directories for .env files. Narrower lookup would reduce unintended secret loading. confidence=0.78. confidence_reasoning=The loop and dotenv calls are visible in the loader.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The loop and dotenv calls are visible in the loader.

content.value_statement: Researchers need clear diagrams for papers, posters, and presentations. This skill turns diagram descriptions into reviewed scientific figures with quality thresholds.
actual_capabilities: Generates scientific diagram images from natural language prompts.
Reviews generated images with a separate vision model.
Applies quality thresholds for journals, posters, talks, theses, and reports.
Writes versioned image files and a review log for each run.
Supports common diagram types such as flowcharts, pathways, circuits, and architectures.
Provides prompt guidance and examples for research figure creation.
limitations: Requires an OpenRouter API key and external network access.
Generated diagrams may still need expert scientific verification.
Output quality depends on the image model response and prompt detail.
The wrapper currently exposes API keys through command-line arguments.

use_cases.title: Create Study Flow Figures
use_cases.description: Draft CONSORT or PRISMA-style participant flow diagrams for manuscripts and posters.

use_cases.title: Explain System Architectures
use_cases.description: Generate architecture and data-flow diagrams for technical reports and presentations.

use_cases.title: Visualize Biological Pathways
use_cases.description: Create pathway and molecular interaction diagrams with labeled components and directed flows.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Diagram
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a single figure.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a simple scientific schematic for [topic]. Include the main components, labels, and flow direction.

prompt_templates.title: Publication Figure
prompt_templates.scenario: Manuscript figure with a document target.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a journal-ready [diagram type] for [study or system]. Use clear labels, high contrast, and a left-to-right layout.

prompt_templates.title: Reviewed Technical Figure
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate and review a [document type] schematic showing [components]. Refine it if the quality score is below the threshold.

prompt_templates.title: Complex Research Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a publication-quality workflow diagram for [process]. Include stages, decision points, counts, annotations, accessibility constraints, and review notes.

output_examples.input: Create a CONSORT participant flow diagram for a randomized clinical trial.
output_examples.output: A versioned flowchart image and a review log with score, critique, and final acceptance status.

output_examples.input: Create a transformer architecture diagram for a conference presentation.
output_examples.output: A labeled architecture figure showing encoder, decoder, attention blocks, and data flow.

output_examples.input: Create a MAPK signaling pathway diagram for a poster.
output_examples.output: A pathway figure with labeled molecules, activation arrows, and a concise quality review.

best_practices: Include the diagram type, components, labels, and preferred flow direction in the prompt.
Use environment variables for API keys and avoid passing secrets as command-line flags.
Review generated figures manually before using them in scientific or clinical material.
anti_patterns: Do not submit vague prompts such as only the topic name.
Do not include private patient, unpublished, or restricted data in prompts.
Do not treat generated scientific content as validated evidence.

faq.question: Does this skill need an API key?
faq.answer: Yes. The scripts require an OpenRouter API key for generation and review requests.

faq.question: Does it make network requests?
faq.answer: Yes. It sends prompts and images to OpenRouter API endpoints for model processing.

faq.question: Can it create publication-ready figures automatically?
faq.answer: It can draft polished figures, but a qualified reviewer should verify scientific accuracy.

faq.question: What diagram types are supported?
faq.answer: Examples include flowcharts, neural network architectures, circuits, pathways, system diagrams, and workflows.

faq.question: Where are results saved?
faq.answer: The scripts save image versions and a review log near the requested output path.

faq.question: Is it safe to pass secrets with the API key flag?
faq.answer: No. Use an environment variable because command-line secrets can be visible to local process observers.
